\chapter*{Introduction}\label{introduction}
\addcontentsline{toc}{chapter}{Introduction}

Statistics is the science of collecting, organising, presenting and analysing data. It is vital in helping us develop a better understanding of the world, and to allow decisions to be made in a rational manner based on the information that is available to us. Governments, organisations, businesses and individuals across a diverse range of fields rely on statistics and statisticians.

\end{center}

The \emph{Advanced Higher Statistics} course aims to equip learners with a sound foundation of statistical knowledge. This textbook has been created to serve as a source of key facts, examples, and exercises to support progress through the course, covering all of the key stages involved in statistical research and investigations, including:

\begin{itemize}
\item
  Gathering data using appropriate \emph{sampling techniques}.
\item
  Initial exploration of data using \emph{descriptive statistics}, including:

  \begin{itemize}
  \item
    Visualising data with charts and graphs.
  \item
    Calculating sample statistics.
  \end{itemize}
\item
  Using \emph{statistical inference} to assess the evidence for claims about population parameters, including:

  \begin{itemize}
  \item
    An introduction to \textit{probability theory}, which underpins all statistical inference
  \item
    Conducting hypothesis tests
  \item
    Constructing confidence intervals
  \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

Techniques suitable for a range of contexts will be covered, and it is expected that by the end of the course students should have sufficient knowledge and confidence to collect their own data and conduct their own statistical investigations.

\chapter{Exploratory Data Analysis}\label{exploratory-data-analysis}

\vspace{-1cm}

Data that has been collected but not yet processed is referred to as \emph{raw data}. Before conducting any statistical analysis, a statistician will typically want to get a quick sense of the data, checking for potential issues and identifying important features. Common first steps are therefore often to: \emph{organise} the data into suitable lists or tables; \emph{visualise} the data using plots and graphs; and \emph{measure} key properties of the data.

This process is called \emph{exploratory data analysis}. It helps us understand the data before applying more formal statistical methods.

\pagebreak

\section{Types of Data}\label{types-of-data}

Knowing what \emph{type} of data has been collected helps determine what kind of graphs and statistics may be appropriate.

Data that is numerical is called \emph{quantitative}, of which there are two types. \textbf{Discrete} data consists of values that can be counted, such as the number of siblings someone has. \textbf{Continuous} data can take \emph{any} value within a given range, though in practice values are often rounded due to measurement precision, such as the mass of a mouse.

\emph{Qualitative} data (also called \textbf{categorical}) is \emph{non-numerical}, such as whether a squirrel is red or grey.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}
\node[draw,fill=black!5,rectangle, text centered,rounded corners,minimum height=0.7cm] at (0,-0.4) (data) {What type of data is it?};
\node[draw,fill=black!5,rectangle, text centered,rounded corners,minimum height=0.7cm] at (-5.5,-2) (qualitative) {Qualitative};
\path[draw,-stealth] (data) -- (0,-1) -- (-5.5,-1) -- (qualitative);
\node[draw,fill=black!5,rectangle, text centered,rounded corners,minimum height=0.7cm] at (6,-2) (quantitative) {Quantitative};
\path[draw,-stealth] (data) -- (0,-1) -- (6,-1) -- (quantitative);
\node[below] at (-3,-1) {data is non-numerical};
\node[below] at (3,-1) {data is numerical};
\node[draw,fill=black!15,rectangle, text centered,rounded corners,minimum height=0.7cm] at (-5.5,-4) (categorical) {\textbf{Categorical}};
\node[draw,fill=black!15,rectangle, text centered,rounded corners,minimum height=0.7cm] at (10,-4) (discrete) {\textbf{Discrete}};
\node[draw,fill=black!15,rectangle, text centered,rounded corners,minimum height=0.7cm] at (2,-4) (continuous) {\textbf{Continuous}};
\path[draw,-stealth] (qualitative) -- (categorical);
\path[draw,-stealth] (quantitative) -- (6,-3) -- (10,-3) -- (discrete);
\path[draw,-stealth] (quantitative) -- (6,-3) -- (2,-3) -- (continuous);
\node[right] at (-5.5,-3) {\textit{also called}};
\node[above] at (8,-3) {countable values};
\node[below] at (8,-3) {\textit{e.g. a list of values}};
\node[below] at (4,-3) {\textit{e.g. a range of values}};
\node[above] at (4,-3) {measureable values};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

For example, the table below shows data relating to some football players:

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex11q}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex11a]{Exercise 1.1}}{Exercise 1.1}}\label{exercise-1.1}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 1.1}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item A survey is carried out on the pets owned by a class. State the type of data that would be collected.
    \begin{enumerate}
    \begin{multicols}{2}
        \item How many pets each pupil owns.
        \item What kind of animal the pets are.
    \end{multicols}
    \end{enumerate}
    \begin{enumerate}[resume]
    \begin{multicols}{2}
        \item The pet’s age.
        \item The name of the pet.
    \end{multicols}
    \end{enumerate}
    \begin{enumerate}[resume]
    \begin{multicols}{2}
        \item The mass of the pet.
        \item Whether the pet is a mammal.
    \end{multicols}
    \end{enumerate}
    \item For each of the following, state whether the quantitative data collected would be discrete or continuous.
    \begin{enumerate}
    \begin{multicols}{2}
        \item The number of people in a room.      
        \item The mass of a book.
    \end{multicols}
    \end{enumerate}
    \begin{enumerate}[resume]
    \begin{multicols}{2}
        \item The number of pages in a book.
        \item The number of songs in an album.
    \end{multicols}
    \end{enumerate}
    \begin{enumerate}[resume]
    \begin{multicols}{2}
        \item The volume of water in a bottle.
        \item Time taken to complete a puzzle.
    \end{multicols}
    \end{enumerate}
    \item A sample of members of a gym were surveyed and the first few results are shown in the table below.
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
      \hline
        Activity level & Smoker & Height (inches) & Mass (pounds) & Pulse (bpm)\\
      \hline
        Moderate & No & 66 & 140 & 64\\
        Moderate & No & 72 & 145 & 58\\
        A lot & Yes & 73.5 & 160 & 62\\
        Slight & Yes & 73 & 190 & 66\\
      \hline
    \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    State the type of data that was collected for each variable.
\end{enumerate}

\pagebreak

\section{Population Parameters and Sample Statistics}\label{population-parameters-and-sample-statistics}

In a statistical study, the term \textbf{population} refers to the entire set of objects of interest, whilst a \textbf{sample} is a subset chosen from the population. Understanding the difference is fundamental to statistics.

\draw[semithick,dashed] (4,4.05) -- (13,2.85);
\draw[semithick,dashed] (11.5,2.75) -- (2,3.45);
\draw[semithick,dashed] (12.25,2.3) -- (7,2.25);
\draw[semithick,dashed] (11,2) -- (2.5,1.05);
\draw[semithick,dashed] (6,0.45) -- (12,1.4);


\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

If data were available for an entire \emph{population}, then various \textbf{population parameters} could be calculated, such as the \emph{population mean}. Since this is rarely possible in practice, instead \emph{samples} are taken from populations. Measures calculated from a sample are called \textbf{sample statistics}, such as the \emph{sample mean}.

Letters from the Greek alphabet are commonly used to represent population parameters. For example:

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{lll}
    \textbf{Population Parameters} & $\qquad $ & \textbf{Sample Statistics}\\[0.2em]
    Population mean $\mu$ \textit{(``mu'')} & $\qquad $ & Sample mean $\bar{x}$ \textit{(``x bar'')}\\[0.1em]
    Population standard deviation $\sigma$ \textit{(``sigma'')}& $\qquad $ & Sample standard deviation $s$\\
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

Sample statistics vary depending on the sample chosen and can be viewed as \emph{estimates} of the true values of their corresponding population parameters, which are treated as fixed (but usually unknown).

\begin{multicols}{2}

\textit{Statistical inference} is the process of using \textit{sample statistics} to assess claims about \textit{population parameters}. This requires learning about the inherent \textit{random variation} that arises when selecting samples from a population. Statistical inference, and \textit{sampling techniques}, will be introduced in later chapters.

\columnbreak

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.8]
\draw[semithick,fill=black!10] (-0.3,2) rectangle (2.7,4);
\node[align=center] at (1.2,4.4) {Population};
\draw[semithick,fill=black!10] (7.8,3) circle (1.1cm);
\node[align=center] at (7.8,4.4) {Sample};
\node[text width=2cm,align=center] at (1.2,3) {Population parameter};
\node[text width = 2cm,align=center] at (7.8,3.1) {Sample statistic};
\draw[very thick,-stealth] (3,3.4)
      arc[start angle=100,end angle=80,radius=10];
\node[black] at (4.8,3.9) {\textit{Sampling}};
\draw[very thick,stealth-] (3,2.8)
      arc[start angle=-100,end angle=-80,radius=10];
\node at (4.8,2.3) {\textit{Inference}};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{multicols}

The reason for discussing sampling at this point is to caution against making \emph{definitive} statements based solely on \emph{sample statistics}, when it is typically the (unknown) \emph{population parameters} that are really of interest.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex12q}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex12a]{Exercise 1.2}}{Exercise 1.2}}\label{exercise-1.2}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 1.2}}

\textit{For each, state using words and notation which sample statistic or population parameter has been obtained.}\newline \textit{For example: ``Sample mean (}\(\bar{x}\)\textit{).'' or ``Population standard deviation (}\(\sigma\)\textit{).''}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Whilst studying house price valuations on a street, the valuations are found of every house on the street, and the mean valuation is calculated.
    \item A bus company in Scotland checks the mileage for some of its buses when they come in for a service, and the mean mileage of these buses is calculated.
    \item The iron levels are checked for a sample of patients at a hospital, and the standard deviation of these is calculated.
    \item Whilst researching the capacities of football stadiums in the Scottish Football League, the standard deviation is calculated using a full list of all the stadiums used in the Scottish Football League.
    \item For a study on lunchtime spending in Scottish schools, the mean lunchtime spend is calculated from surveying every pupil studying as a large secondary school in Edinburgh.
\end{enumerate}

\pagebreak

\section{Measures of Location and Spread}\label{measures-of-location-and-spread}

\emph{Summary statistics} are a collection of measures taken of the data from a sample, such as the mean, or standard deviation. These provide a quick way to communicate some general sense of the data. For example, for the \emph{raw data} of 0, 2, 2, 6, 12, 14, 16, 18, 18, 18, 20, the table below shows a selection of summary statistics.

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{ccccccccccccc}
n & mean & sd & sum & sum of squares & min & LQ & median & UQ & max & range & IQR & mode\\
  \hline
11 & 11.45 & 7.50 & 126 & 2012 & 0 & 2 & 14 & 18 & 20 & 20 & 16 & 18
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

There are two main types of summary statistic: those that measure the \textit{central tendency} of data (or its \textbf{location}); and those that measure the \textit{variability} of data (or its \textbf{spread}). It is common to measure both the location \emph{and} spread, since two data sets can have the same average but very different variability.

\subsubsection*{Measures of Location}\label{measures-of-location}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{Measures of Location}

The three main measures of location, or \emph{averages}, should already be familiar. To avoid ambiguity, statisticians will typically avoid referring to \emph{``the average''}, and will instead specify exactly \emph{which} average they are referring to:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Mode} - the most frequently occurring value.
    \item \textbf{Median} - the middle value after the data has been put into numerical order.
    \item \textbf{Mean} - the sum of the values divided by the number of values.
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}
\draw[semithick] (0,0) -- (10,0);
\node at (0,-0.5) {0};
\node at (2.5,-0.5) {5};
\node at (5,-0.5) {10};
\node at (7.5,-0.5) {15};
\node at (10,-0.5) {20};
\foreach \x in {0,0.5,...,10}
  \draw (\x,0) -- (\x,-0.2);
\fill[black] (0,0.5) circle (0.07);
\fill[black] (1,0.5) circle (0.07);
\fill[black] (1,1) circle (0.07);
\fill[black] (3,0.5) circle (0.07);
\fill[black] (6,0.5) circle (0.07);
\fill[black] (7,0.5) circle (0.07);
\fill[black] (8,0.5) circle (0.07);
\fill[black] (9,0.5) circle (0.07);
\fill[black] (9,1) circle (0.07);
\fill[black] (9,1.5) circle (0.07);
\fill[black] (10,0.5) circle (0.07);
\node at (5.7,1.5) {\text{mean}};
\node at (7,1) {\text{median}};
\node at (9,2) {\text{mode}};
\draw[dashed] (5.7,1.2) -- (5.7,0);
\draw[dashed] (7,0.7) -- (7,0);
\draw[dashed] (9,1.7) -- (9,0);
\node at (5.7,-1) {standard deviation};
\draw[stealth-stealth] (3.95,-1.3) -- (7.45,-1.3);
\node at (5,-2) {interquartile range};
\draw[stealth-stealth] (1,-2.3) --(9,-2.3);
\node at (13,1.2) {\textit{measures of location}};
\node at (13,-1.75) {\textit{measures of spread}};
\node at (13,-2.25) {\textit{(illustrative only)}};
\draw[decoration={brace,mirror,raise=2pt},decorate]
  (11,0.4) -- (11,2);
\draw[decoration={brace,mirror,raise=2pt},decorate]
  (11,-2.5) -- (11,-1);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\subsubsection*{Measures of Spread}\label{measures-of-spread}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{Measures of Spread}

The measure of spread which is usually encountered first in a maths classroom is the \emph{range}, less informative than other measures, as it depends only on the two most extreme values. In practice this measure is seldom used by statisticians since it only uses the two most extreme values from the data. There are three main measures of spread commonly used, two of which are likely to already be familiar:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Interquartile Range} - the difference between the upper quartile and the lower quartile.
    \item \textbf{Variance} - the \textit{mean of the squared differences} between each value and the mean.
    \item \textbf{Standard deviation} - the square root of the variance.
\end{itemize}

\pagebreak

\subsection*{The Mean and the Standard Deviation}\label{the-mean-and-the-standard-deviation}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsection}{The Mean and the Standard Deviation}

The \emph{mean} and \emph{standard deviation} are perhaps the most commonly used measures of location and spread, respectively, and both link strongly to the commonly-encountered \emph{bell-shaped curve} (see page 13). The formulae for calculating the sample mean and sample standard deviation from sample data are given below using notation, where the Greek letter \(\sum\) (\emph{``Sigma''}) denotes summation (i.e.~adding a set of values), \(x_i\) represents each value in the sample (for \(i=\text{1},\text{2},\dots,n\)) and \(n\) represents the \emph{number} of values in the sample.

The \emph{sample mean}, \(\bar{x}\), is calculated as: \hspace{0.5cm}\(\bar{x}=\dfrac{\sum x}{n}\)

The \emph{sample standard deviation}, \(s\), is calculated as: \hspace{0.5cm}\(s=\sqrt{\dfrac{S_{xx}}{n-1}}\)\hspace{0.5cm}where: \hspace{0.5cm}\(S_{xx}=\sum x^\text{2}-\dfrac{(\sum x)^\text{2}}{n}\)

The \emph{sample variance}, \(s^\text{2}\), is simply the \emph{square of the standard deviation}.

\subsubsection*{The Median and the Interquartile Range}\label{the-median-and-the-interquartile-range}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{The Median and the Interquartile Range}

The median, \(\text{Q}_\text{2}\), and interquartile range, \(\text{IQR}\), are often chosen to measure location and spread when the data is particularly \emph{skewed} (see page 13), as they are less influenced by \emph{extreme values} than the mean and standard deviation.

The \emph{lower quartile} (LQ or \(\text{Q}_\text{1}\)) and the \emph{upper quartile} (UQ or \(\text{Q}_\text{3}\)) split an ordered data set into four equal parts, along with the median. From the lowest value to \(\text{Q}_\text{1}\) contains approximately 25\% of the data, from \(\text{Q}_\text{1}\) to \(\text{Q}_\text{2}\) contains approximately the next 25\%, and so on.

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}
\node at (1,0) {4};
\node at (2,0) {5};
\node at (3,0) {8};
\node at (4,0) {8};
\node at (5,0) {10};
\node at (6,0) {11};
\node at (7,0) {13};
\node at (8,0) {17};
\node at (9,0) {17};
\node at (10,0) {18};
\node at (11,0) {21};
\node at (12,0) {22};
\node at (13,0) {22};
\node at (14,0) {26};
\draw[thick] (7.5,-0.5) -- (7.5,0.5) node[above] {$\text{Q}_\text{2}=\text{15}$};
\draw[thick] (4,0) circle (0.5);
\draw[thick] (12,0) circle (0.5);
\node[above] at (4,0.5) {$\text{Q}_\text{1}=\text{8}$};
\node[above] at (12,0.5) {$\text{Q}_\text{3}=\text{22}$};
\draw[stealth-] (0.7,0.3) -- (0.3,0.6) node[above left] {$\text{Min}=\text{4}$};
\draw[stealth-] (14.3,0.3) -- (14.6,0.6) node[above right] {$\text{Max}=\text{26}$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

The \emph{interquartile range} is calculated as: \hspace{0.5cm}\(\text{IQR}=\text{Q}_\text{3}-\text{Q}_\text{1}\)

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex13q}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex13a]{Exercise 1.3}}{Exercise 1.3}}\label{exercise-1.3}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 1.3}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item The monthly cost of 10 drivers' car insurance ($x$, in £'s) are recorded. Calculate the mean and standard deviation.
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{ccc}
      $\sum{x}=\text{359}$ & $\sum{x^{\text{2}}}=\text{15119}$ & $n=\text{10}$
    \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    \item Daily fuel spend ($x$, in £'s) for 15 drivers is summarised below. Calculate the mean and standard deviation.
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{ccc}
      $\sum{x}=\text{87}$ & $\sum{x^{\text{2}}}=\text{569}$ & $n=\text{15}$
    \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    \item The marks, out of 40, of eight pupils sitting a chemistry test are below. Find the mean and standard deviation.
    \begin{center}
        \begin{tabular}{cccccccc}
            32 & 25 & 29 & 37 & 16 & 39 & 23 & 32 
        \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    \item The ages (in years) of 18 customers in a shop are recorded. Calculate the median and interquartile range.
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{ccccccccc}
        17 & 63 & 34 & 23 & 40 & 57 & 72 & 70 & 43\\
        56 & 29 & 37 & 41 & 50 & 13 & 24 & 30 & 58
    \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
\end{enumerate}

\pagebreak

\section{Displaying Data}\label{displaying-data}

Before analysing a data set, it is common practice to \emph{display} the data in a simple form so that an initial impression can be formed. Some key examples are shown below, using a sample of 10 sunflower heights measured by a gardener 8 weeks after germination, measured to the nearest centimetre:

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{cccccccccccc}
34 & 35 & 38 & 41 & 46 & 47 & 47 & 51 & 62 & 73
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\vspace{-0.5cm}

\begin{figure}[H]
  \centering

  \centering

\node at (0.15*50,-0.3) {50};
\node at (0.15*55,-0.3) {55};
\node at (0.15*60,-0.3) {60};
\node at (0.15*65,-0.3) {65};
\node at (0.15*70,-0.3) {70};
\foreach \x in {34,...,73}
  \draw (0.15*\x,0) -- (0.15*\x,-0.1);
\fill[black] (0.15*34,0.4) circle (0.05);

\node[text width = 8cm,below] at (8,4) {Histograms display continuous data grouped into intervals (bins). Unlike bar charts, the bars are adjacent (no gaps), and the area of each bar represents frequency.};
\draw[white] (3.5,-2) rectangle (12.5,5);
\end{tikzpicture}

\end{minipage}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex14a]{Exercise 1.4}}{Exercise 1.4}}\label{exercise-1.4}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 1.4}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item The ages (in years) of competitors in a junior golf tournament are below. Draw a dot plot to represent this data.
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{cccccccccccccc}
        16 & 14 & 19 & 13 & 14 & 17 & 16 & 19 & 16 & 17 & 12 & 16 & 14 & 19
    \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    \item The price of an ice cream is recorded at 12 stalls (in £). Represent the data using a stem-and-leaf diagram.
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{cccccc}
        1.50 & 2.20 & 2.50 & 2.10 & 1.80 & 1.40\\
        2.00 & 1.90 & 3.60 & 0.90 & 1.70 & 1.50
    \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    \item The masses (in kg) of the luggage of 10 passengers on a plane to Barra were recorded. Construct a box plot.
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{cccccccccc}
        15 & 18 & 14 & 22 & 19 & 14 & 10 & 21 & 17 & 16  
    \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
\end{enumerate}

\pagebreak

\section{Tables, Figures and Output}\label{tables-figures-and-output}

There are many ways in which data can be presented, extending beyond those covered on the previous page. In statistical reports they are often clearly labelled as \textbf{tables} or \textbf{figures}, and numbered so they can be more easily referred to.

\begin{multicols}{3}

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}
        \draw[white] (0,0) rectangle (5,4);
        \node[above, text width=5cm] at (2.5,4) {\textbf{Table 1:} Contingency table of mode of transport.}; 
        \draw (0,0) rectangle (0.8,2.4);
        \node[rotate=90] at (0.4,1.2) {Year Group};
        \draw[step=0.8cm] (0.8,0) grid (4.8,2.4);
        \draw[step=0.8cm] (1.6,2.4) grid (4.8,3.2);
        \draw (1.6,3.2) rectangle (4.8,4);
        \node at (3.2,3.6) {Transport};
        \node[rotate=45] at (2,2.8) {\small{walk}};
        \node[rotate=45] at (2.8,2.8) {\small{bus}};
        \node[rotate=45] at (3.6,2.8) {\small{car}};
        \node[rotate=45] at (4.4,2.8) {\small{bike}};
        \node at (1.2,2) {3rd};
        \node at (1.2,1.2) {2nd};
        \node at (1.2,0.4) {1st};
        \node at (2,0.4) {4};
        \node at (2,1.2) {7};
        \node at (2,2) {12};
        \node at (2.8,0.4) {14};
        \node at (2.8,1.2) {13};
        \node at (2.8,2) {15};
        \node at (3.6,0.4) {11};
        \node at (3.6,1.2) {8};
        \node at (3.6,2) {3};
        \node at (4.4,0.4) {2};
        \node at (4.4,1.2) {1};
        \node at (4.4,2) {4};
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}
        \node[above, text width=5cm] at (2.5,4) {\textbf{Figure 1:} Bar chart of mode of transport.}; 
        \draw (0.5,1) -- (0.5,4);
        \node[rotate=90] at (0.2,2.4) {frequency};
        \draw (0.5,1) -- (4.2,1);
        \node at (2.2,0.2) {year group};
        
        \draw[pattern=north west lines] (0.6,1) rectangle (0.8,1.8);
        \draw[fill=black!20] (0.8,1) rectangle (1,3.8);
        \draw (1,1) rectangle (1.2,3.2);
        \draw[fill=black!50] (1.2,1) rectangle (1.4,1.4);
        \node at (1,0.7) {1st};

        \draw[pattern=north west lines] (1.7,1) rectangle (1.9,2.4);
        \draw[fill=black!20] (1.9,1) rectangle (2.1,3.6);
        \draw (2.1,1) rectangle (2.3,2.6);
        \draw[fill=black!50] (2.3,1) rectangle (2.5,1.2);
        \node at (2.1,0.7) {2nd};

        \draw[pattern=north west lines] (2.8,1) rectangle (3,3.4);
        \draw[fill=black!20] (3,1) rectangle (3.2,4);
        \draw (3.2,1) rectangle (3.4,1.6);
        \draw[fill=black!50] (3.4,1) rectangle (3.6,1.8);
        \node at (3.2,0.7) {3rd};
        
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}
        \node[above,text width=5cm] at (2.5,4) {\textbf{Figure 2:} Chart of transport percentages.}; 
        \node at (0.5,3.5) {3rd};
        \node at (0.5,2.7) {2nd};
        \node at (0.5,1.9) {1st};

        \draw[pattern=north west lines] (1,3.2) rectangle (1.7,3.8);
        \draw[fill=black!20] (1.7,3.2) rectangle (2.6,3.8);
        \draw (2.6,3.2) rectangle (2.78,3.8);
        \draw[fill=black!50] (2.78,3.2) rectangle (3,3.8);

        \draw[pattern=north west lines] (1,2.4) rectangle (1.5,3);
        \draw[fill=black!20] (1.5,2.4) rectangle (2.4,3);
        \draw (2.4,2.4) rectangle (2.95,3);
        \draw[fill=black!50] (2.95,2.4) rectangle (3,3);

        \draw[pattern=north west lines] (1,1.6) rectangle (1.26,2.2);
        \draw[fill=black!20] (1.26,1.6) rectangle (2.16,2.2);
        \draw (2.16,1.6) rectangle (2.86,2.2);
        \draw[fill=black!50] (2.86,1.6) rectangle (3,2.2);

        \draw (1,1.4) -- (3,1.4);
        \draw (1,1.3) -- (1,1.4) node[below] {0\%}; 
        \draw (3,1.3) -- (3,1.4) node[below] {100\%}; 
        \draw (2,1.3) -- (2,1.4);
        \draw (1.5,1.3) -- (1.5,1.4);
        \draw (2.5,1.3) -- (2.5,1.4);

        \draw[pattern=north west lines] (0.5,0.75) rectangle (0.75,0.5);
        \draw[fill=black!20] (1.75,0.75) rectangle (2,0.5);
        \draw (3,0.75) rectangle (3.25,0.5);
        \draw[fill=black!50] (4.25,0.75) rectangle (4.5,0.5);

        \node at (0.625,0.25) {walk};
        \node at (1.875,0.25) {bus};
        \node at (3.125,0.2) {car};
        \node at (4.375,0.25) {bike};
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{multicols}

Not all graphs encountered in real life will be up to scratch; some graphs may be unclear, accidentally misleading or even intentionally misleading. Being alert to potential flaws in tables and figures and identifying possible improvements is an important skill.

The widespread availability of computing power means that specialist software is often used by statisticians to avoid the need for time-consuming calculations where possible. Whilst this course requires no use of statistical software, examples of computer \textbf{output} will be displayed from which the required information must be extracted.

\begin{multicols}{2}

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}
        \node[above, text width=7cm] at (3.5,4) {\textbf{Figure 3:} Dot plots of time (mins) to walk to school for 2nd and 3rd years.}; 
        \draw (0,3) -- (5,3);
        \foreach \x in {0,0.5,...,5}{
        \draw (\x,2.8) -- (\x,3);
        }
        \node[below] at (0,2.9) {0};
        \node[below] at (1,2.9) {10};
        \node[below] at (2,2.9) {20};
        \node[below] at (3,2.9) {30};
        \node[below] at (4,2.9) {40};
        \node[below] at (5,2.9) {50};
        \draw (0,1) -- (5,1);
        \foreach \x in {0,0.5,...,5}{
        \draw (\x,0.8) -- (\x,1);
        }
        \node[below] at (0,0.9) {0};
        \node[below] at (1,0.9) {10};
        \node[below] at (2,0.9) {20};
        \node[below] at (3,0.9) {30};
        \node[below] at (4,0.9) {40};
        \node[below] at (5,0.9) {50};

        \node at (6,3.5) {3rd years};
        \node at (6,1.5) {2nd years};
        \draw[fill] (0.6,3.3) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (1.0,3.3) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (1.0,3.5) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (1.3,3.3) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (1.3,3.5) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (1.3,3.7) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (1.7,3.3) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (1.9,3.3) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (2.2,3.3) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (2.2,3.5) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (2.7,3.3) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (3.6,3.3) circle (2pt);

        \draw[fill] (0.3,1.3) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (0.8,1.3) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (1.3,1.3) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (1.7,1.3) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (1.7,1.5) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (2.0,1.3) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (2.6,1.3) circle (2pt);
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\columnbreak

\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}
        \node[above, text width=7cm] at (3.5,4) {\textbf{Output 1:} Summary data for walk times (mins) for 2nd and 3rd years.}; 
        \node[right] at (0,3.5) {\texttt{data: walk time}};
        \node[right] at (0,2.7) {\texttt{3rd yr:  n=12}};
        \node[right] at (0,1.9) {\texttt{2nd yr:  n=****}};
        \node[right] at (3.2,2.7) {\texttt{mean=17.3}};
        \node[right] at (3.2,1.9) {\texttt{mean=14.9}};
        \node[right] at (5.5,2.7) {\texttt{sd=8.42}};
        \node[right] at (5.5,1.9) {\texttt{sd=7.65}};
        \node[right] at (0,1.1) {\texttt{alternative hypothesis: means are not equal}};
        \node[right] at (0,0.3) {\texttt{t=0.619, df=17, p-value=0.544}};
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\end{multicols}

Whilst this textbook aims to enable familiarity with common figures, tables and computer output, the best way to practise obtaining information from them is to \emph{read a wide range of relevant statistical articles and reports}.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex15q}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex15a]{Exercise 1.5}}{Exercise 1.5}}\label{exercise-1.5}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 1.5}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Use \textbf{Table 1} to calculate the proportion of pupils in the sample that walk to school.
    \item Suggest two possible improvements to \textbf{Figure 1}.
    \item Using \textbf{Figure 2}, compare the proportions of pupils in the sample who take the bus between each year group.
    \item From \textbf{Figure 3}, state the approximate median time taken to walk for 3rd year pupils.
    \item One piece of information from \textbf{Output 1} has been replaced by \texttt{****}. State the missing value.
\end{enumerate}

\pagebreak

\section{Outliers and Fences}\label{outliers-and-fences}

Sometimes a set of data may contain values that seem \emph{extremely high or low} in comparison to the rest of the values. It is important to be wary of such values and the potentially significant impact they could have on any statistical analysis performed. There are a range of objective approaches that can be used to determine whether one or more points are extreme enough to be classified as \textbf{outliers}. In this course, \emph{fences} will be used.

\begin{tcolorbox}[center,title=Identifying Outliers,colback=red!5,colframe=red!70!black,width=15cm,halign = center,halign title = center]
\begin{center}
Lower Fence $=\text{Q}_\text{1}-\text{1}.\text{5}\times \text{IQR}$.\\[1em]
Upper Fence $=\text{Q}_\text{3}+\text{1}.\text{5}\times \text{IQR}$.\\[1em]
Any values \textit{below the lower fence} or \textit{above the upper fence} are \textbf{outliers}.
\end{center}
\end{tcolorbox}

If an outlier is identified in a set of data, an investigation should be conducted to determine the cause. If it is concluded that the outlier is a rogue point, such as an error in measurement, it can be removed from the data set and summary statistics re-calculated. However, if an outlier is a valid point in the data set then it should not be removed.

Returning to the sunflower heights from page 6, the data can now be checked for possible outliers:

\begin{multicols}{2}

\begin{center}
\textbf{Raw data}\\[0.8em]
Heights (cm)
\begin{tabular}{cccccccccccc}
34 & 35 & 38 & 41 & 46 & 47 & 47 & 51 & 62 & 73
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\columnbreak

\begin{center}
\textbf{Summary data}\\[1em]
\begin{tabular}{ccccccccccccc}
n & min & Q1 & median & Q3 & max\\
  \hline
10 & 34 & 38 & 46.5 & 51 & 73
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\end{multicols}

\vspace{-1cm}

\begin{align*}
  \text{IQR}&=\text{Q}_\text{3}-\text{Q}_\text{1}=51-38=13\\[1em]
  \text{Lower fence}&=\text{Q}_\text{1}-\text{1}.\text{5}\times \text{IQR}=38-\text{1}.\text{5}\times 13=18.5\\[1em]
  \text{Upper fence}&=\text{Q}_\text{3}+\text{1}.\text{5}\times \text{IQR}=51+\text{1}.\text{5}\times 13=70.5
\end{align*}

Since \(\text{73}>\text{70}.\text{5}\), it is an outlier.

On a box plot, outliers are indicated separately as shown below, with the ``whiskers'' extend only as far as the largest and smallest values that are \emph{not} outliers. However the quartiles remain as those calculated using the \emph{full} set of values, including any outliers. \emph{Plotting outliers separately in this way is to highlight their presence, and is not a judgement on whether those values are rogue or valid.}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}
\node at (0.25*53,2.3) {\text{Height of sunflower plants}};
\node at (0.25*53,-0.2) {\text{Height (cm)}};
\draw[semithick] (33.5*0.25,0.8) -- (73.5*0.25,0.8);
\foreach \x in {34,...,73}
  \draw (0.25*\x,0.8) -- (0.25*\x,0.6);
\node at (0.25*35,0.3) {35};
\node at (0.25*40,0.3) {40};
\node at (0.25*45,0.3) {45};
\node at (0.25*50,0.3) {50};
\node at (0.25*55,0.3) {55};
\node at (0.25*60,0.3) {60};
\node at (0.25*65,0.3) {65};
\node at (0.25*70,0.3) {70};
\fill[black!15] (38*0.25,1.2) rectangle (51*0.25,1.8);
\draw[semithick] (34*0.25,1.3) -- (34*0.25,1.7);
\draw[semithick] (38*0.25,1.2) -- (38*0.25,1.8);
\draw[semithick] (46.5*0.25,1.2) -- (46.5*0.25,1.8);
\draw[semithick] (51*0.25,1.2) -- (51*0.25,1.8);
\draw[semithick] (62*0.25,1.3) -- (62*0.25,1.7);
\draw[semithick] (34*0.25,1.5) -- (38*0.25,1.5);
\draw[semithick] (38*0.25,1.2) -- (51*0.25,1.2);
\draw[semithick] (38*0.25,1.8) -- (51*0.25,1.8);
\draw[semithick] (51*0.25,1.5) -- (62*0.25,1.5);
\draw[semithick] (73*0.25,1.5) circle (0.08);
\draw[stealth-] (18.5,1.75) -- (19,2.25) node[above right] {Outlier};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

The outlier in this data set could be valid if, for instance, the sunflower plant is in a particular sunny spot in the garden. However, if the height was measured incorrectly then it can be removed from the data set.

\pagebreak

\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!,colframe=NavyBlue]
\textbf{Problem:} A vet conducts a survey, asking 10 randomly chosen cat owners registered with his practice to share the mass of their adult cat, in kilograms. The results are:
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{cccccccccc}
    4.6 & 3.6 & 4.1 & 4.5 & 3.7 & 3.9 & 4.0 & 0.6 & 3.9 & 4.8\\
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item[(a)] Show that the set of data contains an outlier.
    \item[(b)] Suggest a possible cause for this outlier which would mean it should be removed.
\end{enumerate}

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution:}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item[(a)] $\text{Q}_\text{1}=\text{3.7}$,\hspace{0.2cm} $\text{Q}_\text{3}=\text{4.5}$,\hspace{0.2cm} $\text{IQR}=\text{4.5}-\text{3.7}=\text{0.8}$.\\[0.5em]
    Lower fence $=\text{3.7}-\text{1}.\text{5}\times \text{0.8}=\text{2.5}$\\[0.5em]
    Upper fence $=\text{4.5}+\text{1}.\text{5}\times \text{0.8}=\text{5.7}$\\[0.5em]
    Since $\text{0.6}<\text{2.5}$, the value of $\text{0.6}$ is an outlier.
    \item[(b)] The owner could have mistakenly measured the mass of their cat in stones instead of kilograms.
\end{enumerate}

\end{tcolorbox}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex16q}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex16a]{Exercise 1.6}}{Exercise 1.6}}\label{exercise-1.6}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 1.6}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Strawberry tarts are sold every day in a bakery over the summer months. The shop manager monitors the sales and takes a sample of the number of strawberry tarts sold on 10 randomly selected days over one month.
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{cccccccccc}
        23 & 31 & 45 & 32 & 35 & 19 & 24 & 25 & 30 & 38
    \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    Calculate the upper and lower fences for this data set and identify any possible outliers.
    \item The number of hours spent studying each week by a randomly selected group of 24 students is given below.
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{cccccccccccc}
        10 & 18 & 15 & 12 & 17 & 27 & 4 & 14 & 14 & 14 & 17 & 15\\
        15 & 12 & 17 & 17 & 13 & 15 & 15 & 16 & 16 & 9 & 11 & 15
    \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Identify any possible outliers.
        \item Draw a box plot to display this data.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item A hospital trust compiled data relating to the length of stay of patients in a particular hospital. A random sample of 18 patients gave the following summary data, measured in days.
    \begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{cccccccc}
        n & mean & sd & min & Q1 & median & Q3 & max\\
      \hline
        18 & 10.78 & 11.17 & 1 & 4 & 7 & 13 & 47
    \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    \vspace{-0.3cm}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}
\node at (0.25*25,-0.4) {\text{Length in days}};
\draw[semithick] (0*0.25,0.4) -- (50*0.25,0.4);
\foreach \x in {0,...,25}
  \draw (0.5*\x,0.4) -- (0.5*\x,0.3);
\node at (0.25*0,0.1) {0};
\node at (0.25*10,0.1) {10};
\node at (0.25*20,0.1) {20};
\node at (0.25*30,0.1) {30};
\node at (0.25*40,0.1) {40};
\node at (0.25*50,0.1) {50};
\fill[black!15] (4*0.25,0.5) rectangle (13*0.25,1);
\draw[semithick] (1*0.25,0.6) -- (1*0.25,0.9);
\draw[semithick] (4*0.25,0.5) -- (4*0.25,1);
\draw[semithick] (7*0.25,0.5) -- (7*0.25,1);
\draw[semithick] (13*0.25,0.5) -- (13*0.25,1);
\draw[semithick] (17*0.25,0.6) -- (17*0.25,0.9);
\draw[semithick] (1*0.25,0.75) -- (4*0.25,0.75);
\draw[semithick] (4*0.25,0.5) -- (13*0.25,0.5);
\draw[semithick] (4*0.25,1) -- (13*0.25,1);
\draw[semithick] (13*0.25,0.75) -- (17*0.25,0.75);
\draw[semithick] (28*0.25,0.75) circle (0.06);
\draw[semithick] (47*0.25,0.75) circle (0.06);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item[(a) ] Show that the two values indicated by circles on the box plot are outliers.
        \item[(b) ] Suggest a cause for these outliers that would make it valid to remove them from further analysis.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}

\pagebreak

\section{Describing Data Distributions}\label{describing-data-distributions}

Summary statistics alone are not sufficient to describe the full complexity of the way data is distributed. It is also important to consider and be able to describe the \textbf{shape} of the distribution, accomplished most easily using a histogram. Whilst real-life data rarely follows a precise mathematical model \emph{exactly}, a number of shapes are commonly observed. The following data was generated by \emph{simulating random sampling} from populations with these distributions.

\draw (3.1,-0.7) -- (3.1,-0.3);
\end{tikzpicture}

\end{minipage}
\end{figure}

\draw (4.6,-0.6) -- (4.6,-0.4);

\end{tikzpicture}

\end{minipage}
\end{figure}

Note that the histograms above were made by selecting some of the most \emph{well-behaved} random samples. Even when data is drawn from a particular distribution, histograms of this sample data may not appear perfectly shaped, especially for smaller sample sizes. Graphs of sample data do not \textbf{show} what the shape, location or spread of distribution of the underlying population is; instead, they merely suggest what may or may not be plausible. Any overly definitive conclusion from a visual representation of sample data should be avoided.

\pagebreak

As well as considering whether the \textbf{underlying population distribution} of data appears to be \emph{normal}, \emph{skewed}, \emph{uniform} or \emph{bimodal}, the appropriateness of some statistical inference techniques will depend on whether the distribution is \emph{symmetrical}, or whether two sets of data follow the \emph{same distribution}, with matching shapes and spread. Whilst the following example references techniques that will be introduced later in the course, it is intended to give an idea of the importance of studying distributions in selecting appropriate methods for statistical inference.

\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!,colframe=NavyBlue]
\textbf{Problem:} An environmental scientist collected water samples from a number of rivers in a region to explore whether the concentration of a harmful chemical in the water has changed since farming practices used locally have changed. They are considering the following choices of hypothesis test:

\begin{itemize}
    \item A \textit{t-test}, the most appropriate choice if the data follows a normal distribution.
    \item A \textit{Wilcoxon Signed Rank test}, appropriate if the data is distributed symmetrically.
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}
\node[text width = 6cm,align=center] at (10,1) {\textbf{Figure 1:} Concentration\\of chemical in river sites};
\draw (-0.5,0) -- (7,0);
\draw (-0.5,0) -- (-0.5,2);
\foreach \x in {-0.5,1,...,7}{
\draw (\x,0) -- (\x,-0.1);
}
\foreach \y in {0,0.4,...,2}{
\draw (-0.5,\y) -- (-0.6,\y);
}
\node[anchor=east] at (-0.6,0) {0};
\node[anchor=east] at (-0.6,0.4) {1};
\node[anchor=east] at (-0.6,0.8) {2};
\node[anchor=east] at (-0.6,1.2) {3};
\node[anchor=east] at (-0.6,1.6) {4};
\node[anchor=east] at (-0.6,2) {5};
\node[anchor=north] at (-0.5,-0.1) {0};
\node[anchor=north] at (1,-0.1) {0.2};
\node[anchor=north] at (2.5,-0.1) {0.4};
\node[anchor=north] at (4,-0.1) {0.6};
\node[anchor=north] at (5.5,-0.1) {0.8};
\node[anchor=north] at (7,-0.1) {1};
\draw[fill=black!25] (-0.5,0) rectangle (0.25,0.4);
\draw[fill=black!25] (0.25,0) rectangle (1,1.4);
\draw[fill=black!25] (1,0) rectangle (1.75,1.9);
\draw[fill=black!25] (1.75,0) rectangle (2.5,1.5);
\draw[fill=black!25] (2.5,0) rectangle (3.25,0.9);
\draw[fill=black!25] (3.25,0) rectangle (4,0.7);
\draw[fill=black!25] (4,0) rectangle (4.75,0.8);
\draw[fill=black!25] (4.75,0) rectangle (5.5,1.6);
\draw[fill=black!25] (5.5,0) rectangle (6.25,1.8);
\draw[fill=black!25] (6.25,0) rectangle (7,0.5);
\node at (3,-0.8) {Concentration (mg/L)};
\node at (-2,1) {Frequency};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item[(a)] Use \textbf{Figure 1} to make a comment on the distribution of chemical concentrations.
    \item[(b)] Comment on the appropriateness of each choice of hypothesis test suggested for this study.
\end{enumerate}

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution:}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item[(a)] Figure 1 suggests that the population distribution of concentrations of the chemical in river sites may be bimodal.
    \item[(b)] Since the histogram suggests the population distribution of chemical concentration may be symmetrical but not normal, a Wilcoxon Signed Rank test would be appropriate whilst a \textit{t}-test would not.
\end{enumerate}

\end{tcolorbox}

\pagebreak

\section{Comparing Distributions}\label{comparing-distributions}

When comparing two or more samples of data, a good starting point is to create suitable plots and obtain summary statistics. Differences and similarities should be noted, considering:

\begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Location} (i.e. means, medians, or clear visual differences in locations)
    \item \textbf{Spread} (i.e. standard deviations, interquartile ranges, or clear visual differences in spread)
    \item \textbf{Shapes} (i.e. symmetrical, skewed, bimodal, bell-shaped curve)
\end{itemize}

At this \emph{exploratory} stage, it is again important to avoid making and \emph{conclusions} relating to the populations from which the sample data was drawn.

\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!,colframe=NavyBlue]
\textbf{Problem:} Within the sport of professional cycling, it is common for riders to specialise in certain types of cycling. \textit{Sprinters} aim to win races that finish on flat roads at high speed, whilst \textit{climbers} aim to win races with an uphill finish. A researcher studying the characteristics of elite-level cyclists randomly selects samples of 30 \textit{sprinters} and 30 \textit{climbers}, recording the heights of each. The results are shown summarised in \textbf{Table 1}.

\vspace{-0.3cm}

\begin{center}
\textbf{Table 1}\\\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{tabular}{cccccccc}
      variable & n & Min & Q1 & Median & Q3 & Max & IQR\\
    \hline
      sprinters & 30 & 165 & 174 & 179 & 184 & 200 & 10\\
      climbers & 30 & 158 & 168 & 174 & 177 & 195 & 9
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

Box plots for the two groups of cyclists are shown below in \textbf{Figure 1}, and dot plots in \textbf{Figure 2}.

\begin{center}
\textbf{Figure 1}\\\vspace{0.2cm}
\begin{tikzpicture}
\node at (0.25*175,-0.2) {\text{Height (cm)}};
\draw[semithick] (150*0.25,0.8) -- (200*0.25,0.8);
\foreach \x in {0,...,23}
  \draw (38.25+0.5*\x,0.8) -- (38.25+0.5*\x,0.6);

\node at (0.25*165,0.3) {170};
\node at (0.25*175,0.3) {180};
\node at (0.25*185,0.3) {190};

\node at (145*0.25,2.5) {sprinters};
\node at (145*0.25,1.5) {climbers};

\draw[semithick] (170*0.25,1.5) -- (175*0.25,1.5);
\draw[semithick] (190*0.25,1.5) circle (0.06);

\draw[semithick, fill = black!10] (169*0.25,2.2) rectangle (179*0.25,2.8);
\draw[semithick] (160*0.25,2.3) -- (160*0.25,2.7);
\draw[semithick] (169*0.25,2.2) -- (169*0.25,2.8);
\draw[semithick] (173*0.25,2.2) -- (173*0.25,2.8);
\draw[semithick] (179*0.25,2.2) -- (179*0.25,2.8);
\draw[semithick] (190*0.25,2.3) -- (190*0.25,2.7);
\draw[semithick] (160*0.25,2.5) -- (169*0.25,2.5);
\draw[semithick] (169*0.25,2.2) -- (179*0.25,2.2);

\begin{enumerate}[itemsep=5pt]
    \item[(a) ] Use \textbf{Figure 1} to make two comments comparing the heights of sprinters and climbers.
    \item[(b) ] Use \textbf{Figure 2} to comment on the shape of the distribution of the heights of climbers.
\end{enumerate}

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution:}

\begin{enumerate}[itemsep=5pt]
    \item[(a) ] The sample median height of the sprinters was greater than that of the climbers.\\[0.5em]
The larger interquartile range suggests that the population heights of sprinters are more varied.
    \item[(b) ] The dot plot suggests the underlying distribution of heights for climbers is negatively skewed.
\end{enumerate}

\end{tcolorbox}
