        \end{center}
\end{minipage}%
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[c]{.4\textwidth}

Prediction intervals are always wider than confidence intervals at the same confidence level, because they account for both uncertainty in the regression line *and* variability of individual observations.\\[0.5em]

Note the more darkly shaded region lying closer to the regression line, showing the narrower confidence interval for the same data.\\[0.5em]

A 95\% prediction interval for $Y_i\,|\,x_i$, such as indicated in the diagram, will capture approximately 95\% of individual response values in the long run.

\end{minipage}
\end{figure}

\begin{tcolorbox}[halign title=center,title=A Prediction Interval for an Individual Response,center,colback=red!5,colframe=red!70!black,width=15cm]
A $100(1-\alpha)\%$ \text{PI} for $Y_i\,|\,x_i$ is given by: $\hat{Y}_i\,\pm\,t_{n-2,1-\alpha/2}\times s\sqrt{1+\dfrac{1}{n}+\dfrac{(x_i-\bar{x})^2}{S_{xx}}}$
\end{tcolorbox}

This formula, also given on Page 5 of the Data Booklet, relies also on the errors, \(\varepsilon_i\), being identically and independently distributed as \(\text{N}\left(0,\sigma^2\right)\)

\begin{tcolorbox}[title=Example,colback=RoyalBlue!1!, colframe=RoyalBlue]
\textbf{Problem:} 

The lengths in centimetres ($x$) and masses in kilograms ($y$) of 12 salmon were measured, with the results:

\begin{center}
    \begin{tabular}{ccccc}
        $\Sigma x=30$ & $\Sigma y=540$ & $S_{xx}=12$ & $S_{yy}=4800$ & $S_{xy}=150$
    \end{tabular}
\end{center}

Construct a 95\% prediction interval for the length of salmon with mass 3kg.

\tcblower
\textbf{Solution:}\\

$b=\dfrac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}}=\dfrac{150}{12}=12.5, \quad a=\bar{y}-b\bar{x}=\dfrac{540}{12}-12.5\times \dfrac{30}{12}=13.75 \quad \implies \quad \hat{Y}_i=13.75+12.5x_i$\\[0.5em]

$\hat{Y}=13.75+12.5\times 3=51.75$\\[0.5em]

$\text{SSR}=S_{yy}-\dfrac{(S_{xy})^2}{S_{xx}}=4800-\dfrac{150^2}{12}=2925, \quad s^2=\dfrac{\text{SSR}}{n-2}=\dfrac{2925}{10}=292.5, \quad s=\sqrt{292.5}=17.1$\\[0.5em]

A 95\% PI for $Y\,|\,x=3$ is $51.75\,\pm\,2.228 \times 17.1\times \sqrt{1+\dfrac{1}{12}+\dfrac{(3-2.5)^2}{12}}=51.75\,\pm\,40.03$\\[0.5em]

So a 95\% prediction interval for the observed length of a 3kg salmon is $(11.72\text{cm}\,,\,91.78\text{cm})$.

\end{tcolorbox}

\pagebreak

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex188q}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex189a]{Exercise 18.9}}{Exercise 18.9}}\label{exercise-18.9}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 18.9}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Students sitting a French exam are given a mark for each of the two components: speaking ($x$) and listening ($y$). Data is gathered from a random sample of 12 students, and a regression line obtained.
    \begin{multicols}{2}
    Summary statistics are calculated as:
    \begin{align*}
        \Sigma x &= 157\\[0.5em]
        S_{xx} &= 334.9\\[0.5em]
        s &= 1.45\\[0.5em]
        n&=12
    \end{align*}
    \vfill\null 
\begin{center}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.6]
        \draw[-stealth] (0,0.8) -- (5.2,0.8) node[right] {$x$};
        \draw[-stealth] (0,0.8) -- (0,5.25) node[above] {$y$}; 
        \node at (2.5,-0.6) {speaking};
        \node[above,rotate=90] at (-1.5,2.5) {listening};
        \foreach \x in {5,10,...,20}{
        \draw (0.25*\x,0.8) -- (0.25*\x,0.7) node[below] {\x};
        }
        \foreach \y in {5,10,...,20}{
        \draw (0,0.25*\y) -- (-0.1,0.25*\y) node[left] {\y};
        }
        \draw[thick] (0.25*3,0.25*3*0.578+0.25*04.854) -- (0.25*20,0.25*20*0.578+0.25*4.854) node[right] {$\hat{Y}=4.854+0.578x$};
\draw[fill] (1.75,2.25) circle (0.06);
\draw[fill] (5,3.25) circle (0.06);
\draw[fill] (3,3.25) circle (0.06);
\draw[fill] (4.75,4.25) circle (0.06);
\draw[fill] (1.5,2.25) circle (0.06);
\draw[fill] (4.25,3.5) circle (0.06);
\draw[fill] (4.25,4) circle (0.06);
\draw[fill] (4.5,4) circle (0.06);
\draw[fill] (3,3) circle (0.06);
\draw[fill] (1,1.5) circle (0.06);
\draw[fill] (4,3.75) circle (0.06);
\draw[fill] (2.25,2.25) circle (0.06);
    \end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
    \end{multicols}
    \vspace{-0.6cm}
    A pupil achieves a speaking score of 15. They are yet to complete the listening component.
    \begin{enumerate}
    \item Obtain a 95\% confidence interval for the mean listening score given a speaking score of 15.
    \item Obtain a 95\% prediction interval for the listening score given a speaking score of 15.
\end{enumerate}
    \item 26 randomly selected trees in a forest have their diameter at a set distance from the ground measured, in inches, and the height of each tree also measured, in feet. The equation of the least squares regression line for $x$ on $y$ is obtained, and shown on the scatterplot below.

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Obtain a 99\% confidence interval for the mean height of a tree with diameter 4 inches.
    \item Obtain a 99\% prediction interval for the height of a tree with diameter 4 inches.
\end{enumerate}
    \item A seller on an online auction site has a stock of used digital cameras, all aged somewhere between 2 and 12 years.     Summary statistics for the data are as follows:
    \begin{center}
        \begin{tabular}{ccc}
            $\Sigma x =337.7$ & \quad & $S_{xx}=221.8$\\[0.5em]
            $S_{yy}=44085.2$ & \quad & $S_{xy}=2192.8$
        \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    The equation for regression line was found:
    \begin{center}
        \texttt{shuttercount $=$ 24.22 + 10.36 $\times$ age}
    \end{center}
    \vfill\null
    \end{multicols}
    \vspace{-0.6cm}
    \begin{enumerate}
    \item Obtain a 90\% prediction interval for the shutter count of a 6 year old camera.
    \item Obtain a 90\% confidence interval for the mean shutter count of a 6 year old camera.
\end{enumerate}
    \pagebreak
    \item The top speed reached (in mph) and the lap times (in seconds) are recorded for a sample of drivers on a race track. Computer output related to a linear model constructed using this data is below.\\[0.5em]
    \begin{tabular}{lllllllll}
        \texttt{y on x regression line} & \qquad &\texttt{n} & \qquad &\texttt{s} & \qquad & \texttt{sum of squares for x} & \qquad & \texttt{sigma x}\\
        \texttt{speed = 269.2 - 1.72 $\times$ time} && \texttt{34} && \texttt{8.49} && \texttt{638.6} && \texttt{72.05}
    \end{tabular}\\[0.5em]
    One driver completed their lap in a time of 52 seconds, but their top speed was not recorded. They wish to estimate a range of values for their top speed. Obtain a suitable interval at the 95\% level.

\draw[fill] (5.2,2) circle (0.04);
\draw[fill] (3.2,2) circle (0.04);
\draw[fill] (5.2,2.6) circle (0.04);
\draw[fill] (4.4,2.2) circle (0.04);
\draw[fill] (8.8,5.2) circle (0.04);
\draw[fill] (3,1.8) circle (0.04);
\draw[fill] (4.2,1.9) circle (0.04);
\draw[fill] (3.2,2) circle (0.04);
\draw[fill] (2,1.6) circle (0.04);
\draw[fill] (5.6,2.1) circle (0.04);
\draw[fill] (5.6,3.2) circle (0.04);
\draw[fill] (3.2,2) circle (0.04);
\draw[fill] (4.8,2.4) circle (0.04);
\draw[fill] (2.2,2.7) circle (0.04);
\draw[fill] (5.2,3.3) circle (0.04);
        \end{tikzpicture}
    \end{center}
    A linear model is fit to the data as shown with a regression line equation of:
    \begin{center}
        \texttt{weight = -63.77 + 0.7649 $\times$ height}
    \end{center}
    A footballer, whose weight is unknown, has a height of 190 centimetres.
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Use the equation of the regression line to calculate an estimate for the footballer's weight.
    \end{enumerate}
    Computer output is used to generate a 95\% prediction interval for the weight of a footballer with a height of 190 centimetres.
    \vspace{-0.3cm}
    \begin{center}
        \texttt{95\% PI}\\[0.5em]
        \texttt{(71.59,****)}
    \end{center}
    \vspace{-0.2cm}
    \begin{enumerate}[resume]
        \item Determine the missing upper value for the prediction interval above, indicated by '\texttt{****}'.
        \item State the assumptions about the population of errors required for the predication interval above.
    \end{enumerate}
    Summary statistics are obtained for the data, as shown below:
    \begin{center}
        \begin{tabular}{ccccccc}
            $S_{xx}=4319.0$ & \qquad & $S_{yy}=4122.4$ & \qquad & $S_{xy}=3303.6$ & \qquad & $n=69$
        \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    \begin{enumerate}[resume]
        \item Calculate a confidence interval for the weight of a footballer of height 172cm.
        \item Explain why the method used above may not be appropriate to help estimate the mean weight of a footballer with a height of 158 centimetres.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}

\pagebreak

\protect\phantomsection\label{re18q}{}

\section*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[re18a]{Review Exercise}}{Review Exercise}}\label{review-exercise-17}
\addcontentsline{toc}{section}{{Review Exercise}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item Biologists studying populations of European hamsters record the tail length ($x$ centimetres) and body mass ($y$ grams) of a random sample of 12 wild hamsters found living in central Vienna. The linear regression equation was calculated for the data shown in the scatterplot as $\hat{Y}=-15.24+71.88x$.
    \begin{figure}[H]
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[c]{.22\textwidth}
\begin{center}
        \begin{tikzpicture}
            \draw[black!10,step=0.25] (0,0.5) grid (2,2.5);
            \node[above,rotate=90] at (0,1.5) {mass};
            \node[below] at (1,0.5) {tail length};
            \draw (0,0.5) rectangle (2,2.5);
            \draw[fill] (1.026584,1.738622) circle (0.03);
            \draw[fill] (1.231034,1.644741) circle (0.03);
            \draw[fill] (1.672522,2.274302) circle (0.03);
            \draw[fill] (1.751120,1.537935) circle (0.03);
            \draw[fill] (1.161184,1.750612) circle (0.03);
            \draw[fill] (1.380849,1.824813) circle (0.03);
            \draw[fill] (0.284826,1.080322) circle (0.03);
            \draw[fill] (1.339594,1.190204) circle (0.03);
            \draw[fill] (0.449474,0.8729) circle (0.03);
            \draw[fill] (1.763575,2.009393) circle (0.03);
            \draw[fill] (0.539653,0.934839) circle (0.03);
            \draw[fill] (1.649056,2.056133) circle (0.03);
            \draw (0,0.7228) -- (2,2.1604);
        \end{tikzpicture}
    \end{center}
\end{minipage}%
\begin{minipage}[c]{.78\textwidth}
    \vspace{-0.3cm}
    \begin{center}
        \begin{tabular}{ccccc}
            $\Sigma x=62.25$, & $\Sigma y=4291.5$, & $\Sigma x^2=325.9$, & $\Sigma y^2=1558042$, & $\Sigma xy=22475$
        \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    \vspace{0.2cm}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Calculate the residual of a 410 gram hamster with a tail length of 5.2cm.
        \item Use a suitable hypothesis test to assess the usefulness of the linear model for prediction at the 10\% level of significance.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{minipage}
\end{figure}
    \item Data is collected on the tempo ($T$ beats per minute) and duration ($d$ minutes) of a random sample of pop-punk songs. The regression line $\hat{T}=256.65-20.65d$ is obtained for the data.
    \begin{figure}[H]
  \centering
\begin{minipage}[c]{.22\textwidth}

        \begin{tikzpicture}

            \draw[fill] (0.271909,1.5685) circle (0.03);
\draw[fill] (0.954002,1.003012) circle (0.03);

\draw[fill] (1.173768,1.199898) circle (0.03);
\draw[fill] (0.799466,1.661934) circle (0.03);
\draw (0,1.5135) -- (2,0.6875);
        \end{tikzpicture}
    \end{center}
\end{minipage}%
\begin{minipage}[c]{.78\textwidth}
    \begin{center}
        \begin{tabular}{lllll}
            $\Sigma d=43.6$ & \qquad & $\Sigma T=3719.5$ & \qquad & $\Sigma dT=8947.2$\\
            $\Sigma d^2=108.5$ & \qquad & $\Sigma T^2=772792$ & \qquad & $n=18$
        \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    \vspace{0.2cm}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Calculate the coefficient of determination and interpret its value.
        \item Perform a hypothesis test at the 1\% level of significance to check for evidence of a linear association between the tempo and duration of pop-punk songs.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{minipage}
\end{figure}
\item The creators of a brand new sports website collect data on the annual social media marketing spend (£$x$ thousand) and new daily visitors ($y$) of similar sites, to plan their own budget. The results are shown below, including a least squares regression line of equation $\hat{Y}=29.5+5.6x$.

\begin{minipage}[c]{.78\textwidth}

        \begin{tabular}{lllllllll}
            $\Sigma x=230.6$ & \quad & $\Sigma x^2=2244.6$ & \quad & $S_{xy}=1118.6$ & \quad & $S_{yy}=20640.9$ & \quad & $n=26$
        \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    \vspace{0.2cm}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Calculate an estimate for the error variance.
        \item Calculate a 95\% prediction interval for the daily new visitors of a site with an annual social media marking spend of £15,000.
        \item Explain why a prediction interval is more likely to be of interest to the magazine's creators than a confidence interval.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{minipage}
\end{figure}
    \item The Scottish physicist James Forbes was interested in finding a way to estimate altitude from measurements of the boiling temperature of water. Some of his observations on boiling point ($y$, in $F^{\circ}$) and barometric pressure ($x$, in inches of mercury) are summarised below. After plotting a scatterplot for this data, a least squares regression line was obtained with the equation $\hat{Y}=155.29+1.9018x$, from which a residual plot was also created.

\vspace{-0.2cm}
\begin{enumerate}[resume]
    \item  Comment on the suitability of the linear model.
    \item Suggest what next steps might be taken on the basis of the residual plot for the linear model.

\pagebreak

\begin{enumerate}[resume]
    \item The website \textit{Fife Walking} contains an extensive collection of walking routes grouped by area. The length ($x$ miles) and total vertical ascent ($y$ metres) is shown in \textbf{table 1} below for a random sample of eleven routes in the \textit{Ochil Hills} area.
    \vspace{-0.4cm}
    \begin{center}
        \textbf{Table 1}\\[0.3em]
        \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|}
            \hline
            \textbf{Route Name} & \textbf{Distance (miles)} & \textbf{Ascent (metres)}\\
            \hline
            Beld Hill and Craig Rossie& 6   &460\\\hline
            Dumyat& 3.8 &500\\\hline
            Myretoun Hill and Balquharn Glen&   4   &470\\\hline
            Colsnaur Circuit&   5   &580\\\hline
            Core Hill   &6.5    &480\\\hline
            Eastbow Hills and Steele's Knowe    &6.2    &430\\\hline
            King's Seat Circuit&    5.6&    600\\\hline
            Burn of Sorrow Circular&    6   &700\\\hline
            Hilfoot Hill and Castle Campbell    &3.2&   300\\\hline
            Innerdownie, Whitewisp and Glenquey Circuit&    8&  550\\\hline
            Ben Shee    &5  &365\\
            \hline
        \end{tabular}
    \end{center}
    The data is displayed below in a scatterplot below (\textbf{figure 1}), with the equation of a $y$ on $x$ least squares regression line calculated as $\hat{Y}=304.08+35.25x$.
    \begin{center}
        \textbf{Figure 1}\\[0.3em]
        \begin{tikzpicture}
            \draw (0,0) -- (10,0);
            \draw (0,0) -- (0,8);
            \foreach \x in {0,1,...,10}{
            \draw (\x,0) -- (\x,-0.2) node[below] {\x};
            }
            \foreach \y in {0,100,...,800}{
            \draw (0,0.01*\y) -- (-0.2,0.01*\y) node[left] {\y};
            }
            \node at (5,-1.2) {distance (miles)};
            \node[above,rotate=90] at (-1.2,4) {ascent (metres)};
            \draw[fill] (6,4.6) circle (0.08);
            \draw[fill] (3.8,5) circle (0.08);
            \draw[fill] (4,4.7) circle (0.08);
            \draw[fill] (5,5.8) circle (0.08);
            \draw[fill] (6.5,4.8) circle (0.08);
            \draw[fill] (6.2,4.3) circle (0.08);
            \draw[fill] (5.6,6) circle (0.08);
            \draw[fill] (6,7) circle (0.08);
            \draw[fill] (3.2,3) circle (0.08);
            \draw[fill] (8,5.5) circle (0.08);
            \draw[fill] (5,3.65) circle (0.08);
            \draw (2.5,3.92205) -- (9,6.2133) node[right] {$\hat{Y}=304.08+35.25x$};
        \end{tikzpicture}
    \end{center}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Calculate the correlation coefficient and interpret it, with reference to the context.
        \item Explain why the regression line obtained would not be suitable for estimating the distance a route covers if it is known that the total ascent is 430 metres.
        \item Determine the equation of a more suitable regression line, and use it to estimate the distance covered for a route with an ascent of 430 metres.
        \item A walker completes a route in the Lomond Hills area, which also features on the \textit{Fife Walking} website. Explain why it may not be appropriate for them to use either of the regression lines obtained to predict their total ascent if their distance covered is known.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}

\pagebreak

\markboth{Answers}{ANSWERS}

\subsection*{Chapter 1 Answers - Exploratory Data Analysis}\label{chapter-1-answers---exploratory-data-analysis}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsection}{Chapter 1 Answers - Exploratory Data Analysis}

\begingroup
\footnotesize

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex11a}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex11q]{Exercise 1.1}}{Exercise 1.1}}\label{exercise-1.1-1}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 1.1}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \begin{multicols}{6}
        \item Quantitative
        \item Qualitative
        \item Quantitative
        \item Qualitative
        \item Quantitative
        \item Qualitative
        \end{multicols}
    \end{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \begin{multicols}{6}
        \item Discrete
        \item Continuous
        \item Discrete
        \item Discrete
        \item Continuous
        \item Continuous
        \end{multicols}
    \end{enumerate}
    \item Categorical: \textit{activity level} and \textit{smoker}. Continuous: \textit{height} and \textit{mass}. Discrete: \textit{pulse}.
\end{enumerate}

    \item Sample statistic (sample mean)

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex14a}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex14q]{Exercise 1.4}}{Exercise 1.4}}\label{exercise-1.4-1}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 1.4}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{multicols}{3}
    \begin{tikzpicture}[baseline=(current bounding box.north)]
        \draw (0.6*12,0) -- (0.6*20,0);
        \foreach \x in {12,14,...,20}{
        \node[below] at (0.6*\x,0) {\x};
        }
        \foreach \x in {12,13,...,20}{
        \draw (0.6*\x,0) -- (0.6*\x,-0.1);
        }
        \draw[fill] (0.6*16,0.4) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (0.6*14,0.4) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (0.6*19,0.4) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (0.6*13,0.4) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (0.6*14,0.8) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (0.6*17,0.4) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (0.6*16,0.8) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (0.6*19,0.8) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (0.6*16,1.2) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (0.6*17,0.8) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (0.6*12,0.4) circle (2pt);
        \draw[fill] (0.6*16,1.6) circle (2pt);

    \item The population of hazelnuts masses appear to follow a positively skewed distribution.
    \item The population of tree heights appear to be normally distributed.
    \item The population of waiting times appear to be uniformly distributed.

\end{enumerate}

    The higher interquartile range for judge A shows that their scores were more varied.

        \item The median length of \textit{iris versicolor} petals appears to be greater than the length of \textit{iris setosa} petals. The length of \textit{iris versicolor} petals appears to be more varied than those of the \textit{iris setosa} species, as seen by their interquartile ranges of $0.18$ and $0.60$ centimetres respectively.

        \item $\text{P}(\text{at least one stop})=0.776$
        \item $\text{P}(\text{exactly two stops})=0.252$
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}
\end{multicols}

\subsection*{Chapter 3 Answers - Sampling Theory}\label{chapter-3-answers---sampling-theory}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsection}{Chapter 3 Answers - Sampling Theory}

\begingroup
\footnotesize

\textbf{Note:} Whilst the answers included here are intended to include an appropriate level of detail, variations in the amount and type of information given in exam questions will require a flexible and common-sense approach to be used in any exam. Past exam questions and marking schemes should be used as a guide, as well as course reports and relevant CPD events.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex31a}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex31q]{Exercise 3.1}}{Exercise 3.1}}\label{exercise-3.1-1}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 3.1}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item
        \begin{enumerate}
            \item People who use his local supermarket.
            \item All shoppers who use the self-checkout tills on that Sunday morning.
        \end{enumerate}
        \item The sampling frame excludes people who prefer to shop at other times, such as during the evening or midweek. It also only includes those using self-checkout tills, likely excluding those who have a large trolly of shopping. Both of these issues are likely to make the sample unrepresentative of the target population, and may introduce bias.
        \item The sampling methods is non-random and so will likely introduce bias to the data, making it unsuitable.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item There may be so many flowers that it is too time-consuming and impractical to make a sampling frame comprised of individual flowers.
        \item A better choice of sampling unit for the sampling frame may be square metres of field, forming a numbered grid.
        \item A random number generator will help avoid possible bias in the selection of square metres to include in the sample, by ensuring it is random and not influenced by the botanist. It should help produce a sample that is more representative of the population of wildflowers.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex32a}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex32q]{Exercise 3.2}}{Exercise 3.2}}\label{exercise-3.2-1}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 3.2}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item One-stage cluster sampling.
        \item Stratified sampling.
        \item Systematic sampling.
        \item Simple random sampling.
        \item Quota sampling.
        \item Convenience Sampling.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Each of the different types of room may have their own distinct characteristics in terms of ageing or the quality of maintenance. Stratified sampling will ensure that a proportionate number from each type of room will be included in the sample.
        \item 30 out of 360 cabins means 1 in every 12 cabins should be sampled, so the sample should contain: 2 Deluxe cabins; 6 Premium cabins and 22 Standard cabins. To sample the Deluxe cabins, a numbered list of those cabins should be obtained as a suitable sampling frame, and then a random number generator used to produce 2 different random numbers. Those cabins corresponding to the numbers selected should be included in the sample. This process should then be repeated for the Premium cabins and the Standard cabins, using a numbered sampling frame for each and randomly selecting the appropriate number of cabins of each type. Combining all of the selected cabins will make a stratified sample.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Convenience sampling.
        \item He only samples coral reefs close to his town, ignoring that those near other towns or villages, or rural locations, may have a different level of health. This means the sample is unlikely to be representative of the the population of coral reefs along the West Coast of Scotland. (Alternatively, the non-random selection method may be discussed.)
    \end{enumerate}
    \item
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Using cluster sampling means less physical travelling to a larger number of petrol stations, which would cost more and take more time.
        \item To sample 40 pumps, 5 petrol stations are needed if one-stage cluster sampling is used. A sampling frame of all the petrol stations numbered from 1 to 126 should be drawn up. A random number generator can then be used to select 5 numbers, and the petrol stations corresponding to those numbers should be included in the sample. From each of those petrol stations, all of the pumps should be sampled.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Systematic sampling.
        \item She didn't use a random starting point, so it is not a true random sampling process that is being used.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Quota sampling.
        \item This is a non-random sampling method which is liable to produce a sample not representative of the target population, and may introduce bias into the data gathered. 
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}

\protect\phantomsection\label{re3a}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[re3q]{Chapter 3 Review Exercise}}{Chapter 3 Review Exercise}}\label{chapter-3-review-exercise}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Chapter 3 Review Exercise}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item
        \begin{enumerate}
            \item Convenience sampling.
            \item This is a non-random sampling method which may produce a sample that is not representative of the target population, and may introduce bias to the data.
        \end{enumerate}
        \item
        \begin{enumerate}
            \item Simple random sampling.
            \item With the 150 employees in an ordered list, the random cell selection tool could be used to select the first employee to be included in the sample. From there, every 5th employee on the list could be selected, looping back around to the start of the list if needed until the sample of size 30 has been obtained.
        \end{enumerate}
        \item
        \begin{enumerate}
            \item A numbered list of the restaurants in the chain could be obtained. A random number generator could be used to pick several numbers, and those restaurants corresponding to the numbers could be selected to be sampled. For a one-stage cluster sample, all employees at those restaurants should be sampled.
            \item One advantage of this method is that it will require less restaurants to be visted for an equivalent sample size, reducing travel costs. One disadvantage is that visiting less restaurants may make it more likely than any restaurants in the chain experiencing problems have none of their employees included in the sample.
        \end{enumerate}
    \end{enumerate}
    \item For an overall sample size of 150 out of 5000 (3\%), the sample needs to include 3 people from Management, 12 from Sales and 135 from the Shop Floor. For each strata, an ordered sampling frame should be obtained, and numbers assigned. For the Management sample, a random number generator should be used to choose 3 numbers, and those staff corresponding to those numbers should be selected to be in the sample. The same process should be repeated to choose 12 from the Sales samling frame, and 135 from the Shop Floor sampling frame. Put together, this will make a sample of size 150 with proportions from each strata matching those of the target population.
    \item The pizzas sampled will only be those from takeaway restaurants, and may not be representative of pizzas sold in the UK as a whole. Additionally, only those takeaways with a presence on social media will be included.
    \item
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item It is unlikely to be possible for the researcher to obtain a list of all pupils in Scotland studying Higher Mathematics.
        \item From an ordered list of all centres in Scotland which  presents pupils for Higher Maths, simple random sampling could be used to select a number of schools. For each of those schools selected, an ordered list of all pupils taking Higher Maths should be obtained, and used to selected a random number of pupils from each selected school. Each of those pupils selected should be included in the overall sample.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item A random letter selector and a random number generator could be used together to select a letter and a number, to correspond to grids, such as 'C' and '5' to select grid C5. This process could be repeated to create a list of grids to for the botanist to visit and study the health of flowers in those grids.
\end{enumerate}

\endgroup
\pagebreak

        \item $\text{P}(Y)=\tfrac{4}{9}$

        \item $\text{P}(Y\cup X)=\tfrac{2}{3}$

        \item $\text{P}(\text{3-pointer }|\text{ scored})=\tfrac{15}{73}$

        \item P(does not leave | not rainy)$=0.1$

        \item $\text{P}(B \cup C)=\tfrac{3}{5}$
        \item $\text{P}(A \cap B \cap C)=\tfrac{1}{15}$

        \item $\text{P}(\overline{B} | C)=\tfrac{3}{5}$
        \item $\text{P}(C | \overline{A})=\tfrac{2}{9}$
        \item $\text{P}((A \cap B) | C)=\tfrac{1}{5}$
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}
\end{multicols}

    \item

        \item $V(X-Y)=\tfrac{8}{3}$

\end{multicols}

\subsection*{Chapter 13 - Control Charts}\label{chapter-13---control-charts}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsection}{Chapter 13 - Control Charts}

\begingroup
\footnotesize

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex131a}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex131q]{Exercise 13.1}}{Exercise 13.1}}\label{exercise-13.1-1}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 13.1}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item The process is out of statistical control, since sample 6 falls below the lower control line. The process should have been stopped after sample 6.
    \item The process is out of statistical control, since from samples 4 to 8 at least four of the five samples lie above the upper 1-sigma line. The process should have been stopped after sample 8.
    \item The process is under statistical control.
    \item The process is out of statistical control, since from samples 2 to 9 eight consecutive samples lie above the centre line. The process should have been stopped after sample 9.
\end{enumerate}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex132a}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex132q]{Exercise 13.2}}{Exercise 13.2}}\label{exercise-13.2-1}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 13.2}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item UCL = 82.5, +2$\sigma$ = 81.75, +1$\sigma$ = 80.75, target = 80, -1$\sigma$ = 79.25, -2$\sigma$ = 78.5, LCL = 77.75
        \item UCL = 500.72, +2$\sigma$ = 495.81, +1$\sigma$ = 490.91, target = 486, -1$\sigma$ = 481.09, -2$\sigma$ = 476.19, LCL = 471.28
    \end{enumerate}
    \item $+2\sigma=1.447$, $-2\sigma=1.353$
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $+1\sigma=231.79$, $-1\sigma=228.21$
        \item The process is out of statistical control since, from samples 9 to 11, two of the three samples lie above the upper 2-sigma limit. The process should have been stopped after sample 11.
        \item A required assumption is that the samples of bottles are random.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $144-3\times\dfrac{18}{\sqrt{7}}=123.59$
        \item $130.4\leqslant \bar{x} \leqslant 164.4$
    \end{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item The process is out of statistical control since eight consecutive points lie above the centre line, from samples 9 to 16. However, since these values suggest the lenses being produced have a higher resolution than the target value, which is a good thing, they may not be concerned.
        \item Samples of size 9 are being taken.
        \item Two required assumptions are that:
        \begin{itemize}
            \item The samples of lenses taken are random.
            \item The resolution scores of manufactured lenses are normally distributed.
        \end{itemize}
    \end{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item UCL = 121, +2$\sigma$ = 119, +1$\sigma$ = 117, target = 115, -1$\sigma$ = 113, -2$\sigma$ = 111, LCL = 109.\newline
        The process is out of statistical control since, from samples 2 to 4, two out of three sample means lie above the upper 2-sigma limit.
        \item $\bar{\bar{x}}=92$
        \item UCL = 101, LCL = 83 
    \end{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{multicols}{2}
        \begin{enumerate}
            \item \phantom{ }\\
                \begingroup
                \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.5}
                \begin{tabular}{|r|l|}
                \hline
                    Population mean & 72\\
                    \hline
                    Standard deviation & 15\\
                    \hline
                    Sample size & 25\\
                    \hline
                    \hline
                    Upper Control Limit & 81\\
                    \hline
                    $+2\sigma$ Limit & 78\\
                    \hline
                    $+1\sigma$ Limit &75\\
                    \hline
                    Target Value & 72\\
                    \hline
                    $-1\sigma$ Limit & 69\\
                    \hline
                    $-2\sigma$ Limit & 66\\
                    \hline

                    \hline
                \end{tabular}
                \endgroup
            \item \phantom{ }\\
                \begingroup

                    \hline

                    \hline

\pagebreak

\begingroup
\footnotesize

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex133a}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex133q]{Exercise 13.3}}{Exercise 13.3}}\label{exercise-13.3-1}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 13.3}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{multicols}{2}
        \begin{enumerate}
            \item Population proportion $=0.6$\\
            Sample size $=24$\\[0.2em]
                \begingroup
                \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.5}
                \begin{tabular}{|r|l|}
                \hline
                    Upper Control Limit & 0.9\\
                    \hline
                    $+2\sigma$ Limit & 0.8\\
                    \hline
                    $+1\sigma$ Limit & 0.7\\
                    \hline
                    Target Value & 0.6\\
                    \hline
                    $-1\sigma$ Limit & 0.5\\
                    \hline
                    $-2\sigma$ Limit & 0.4\\
                    \hline
                    Lower Control Limit & 0.3\\
                    \hline
                \end{tabular}
                \endgroup
            \item Population proportion $=0.16$\\
            Sample size $=50$\\[0.2em]
                \begingroup
                \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.5}
                \begin{tabular}{|r|l|}
                \hline
                    Upper Control Limit & 0.316\\
                    \hline
                    $+2\sigma$ Limit & 0.264\\
                    \hline
                    $+1\sigma$ Limit & 0.212\\
                    \hline
                    Target Value & 0.16\\
                    \hline
                    $-1\sigma$ Limit & 0.108\\
                    \hline
                    $-2\sigma$ Limit & 0.056\\
                    \hline
                    Lower Control Limit & 0.004\\
                    \hline
                \end{tabular}
                \endgroup
        \end{enumerate}
    \end{multicols}
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item The samples of mugs would not be random. Also, for appropriate use of a $p$ chart it is required that $np>5$ and $nq>5$. Here, $0.08\times 30=2.4<5$. 
        \item Upper 2-sigma limit = 0.14265 and lower 2-sigma limit = 0.01735.
        \item The process is under statistical control for the samples shown, as none of the WECO rules have been violated.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $0.11+3\times\sqrt{\dfrac{0.11\times 0.89}{50}}=0.2427$
        \item The calculated lower limit would be -0.0227. However, a negative sample proportion is not possible, so the lower control limit is set as 0.
        \item The process is out of statistical control since, from samples 3 to 7, four out of five samples lie below the lower 2-sigma limit.
        \item Since these sample proportions suggest less seeds aere failing to germinate than expected, which is a good thing, the company many not be concerned.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item UCL = 0.9549, LCL = 0.6451.
        \item The process is out of statistical control since, from February to September, eight consecutive sample proportions fall below the target value.
        \item The sample size needs to be at least 30 to ensure both $np>5$ and $nq>5$.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $\hat{p}=0.12$
        \item Upper 2-sigma limit $=0.12+2\times\sqrt{\dfrac{0.12\times0.88}{50}}=0.212$ and lower 2-sigma limit $=0.12-2\times\sqrt{\dfrac{0.12\times0.88}{50}}=0.028$
        \item The process is under statistical control for the samples shown, as none of the WECO rules have been violated.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item The population proportion is $p=0.6$.
        \item UCL = 0.9 and LCL = 0.3.
        \item The sample size is $n=24$.
        \item The sample size and population proportion are appropriate since both of  $24\times 0.6=14.4$ and $24\times 0.4 = 9.6$ are greater than $5$.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}

\protect\phantomsection\label{re13a}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[re13q]{Chapter 13 Review Exercise}}{Chapter 13 Review Exercise}}\label{chapter-13-review-exercise}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Chapter 13 Review Exercise}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Upper 2-sigma limit $=60.12$ and lower 2-sigma limit $=55.88$.
        \item The process is out of statistical control since the sample mean is below the lower control limit at sample 14.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Upper 1-sigma limit $=0.926$ and lower 1-sigma limit $=0.834$.
        \item $0.88\leqslant \hat{p} \leqslant 1$
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex161a}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex161q]{Exercise 16.1}}{Exercise 16.1}}\label{exercise-16.1-1}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 16.1}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item $9>3\implies\text{ do not reject }H_0$
    \item $5.5<3\implies\text{ reject }H_0$
    \item $8>5\implies\text{ do not reject }H_0$
    \item $7.5>5\implies\text{ do not reject }H_0$
    \item $10\leqslant 10\implies\text{ reject }H_0$
    \item $9<10\implies\text{ reject }H_0$
    \item $6.5>5\implies\text{ do not reject }H_0$
\end{enumerate}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex162a}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex162q]{Exercise 16.2}}{Exercise 16.2}}\label{exercise-16.2-1}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 16.2}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item $8.5<13\implies\text{ reject }H_0$
    \item $11>5\implies\text{ do not reject }H_0$
    \item $46>21\implies\text{ do not reject }H_0$
    \item $1.5<2\implies\text{ reject }H_0$
    \item $4<8\implies\text{ reject }H_0$
\end{enumerate}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex163a}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex163q]{Exercise 16.3}}{Exercise 16.3}}\label{exercise-16.3-1}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 16.3}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item $-0.34>-1.96\implies\text{ do not reject }H_0$
    \item $-1.67<-1.64\implies\text{ reject }H_0$
    \item $-0.27>-1.64\implies\text{ do not reject }H_0$
    \item
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $H_0: \eta =110, \quad H_1: \eta<110$
        \item $-2.16>-1.64\implies\text{reject }H_0$
        \item Assume that time taken to complete the hike is symmetrically distributed.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}

\protect\phantomsection\label{re16a}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[re16q]{Chapter 16 Review Exercise}}{Chapter 16 Review Exercise}}\label{chapter-16-review-exercise}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Chapter 16 Review Exercise}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item $5\leqslant5\implies\text{ reject }H_0$
    \item $14>8\implies\text{ do not reject }H_0$
    \item $-2.40>-1.96\implies\text{ reject }H_0$
    \item $3>2\implies\text{do not reject }H_0$
\end{enumerate}

\endgroup

\subsection*{Chapter 17 - Mann Whitney Rank Sum Test}\label{chapter-17---mann-whitney-rank-sum-test}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsection}{Chapter 17 - Mann Whitney Rank Sum Test}

\begingroup
\footnotesize

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex171a}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex171q]{Exercise 17.1}}{Exercise 17.1}}\label{exercise-17.1-1}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 17.1}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item Rank sum of 20.5, or rank sum of 44.5 leading to smaller value of 20.5.
        \item $20.5<21\implies\text{ reject }H_0$
    \end{enumerate}
    \item $24>20\implies\text{ do not reject }H_0$
    \item $73.5< 74\implies\text{ reject }H_0$
    \item $25<31\implies\text{ reject }H_0$ at the 5\% level
    \item $66.5>66\implies\text{ do not reject }H_0$ at the 5\% level
    \item Assume distributions of learning times for each type of mouse have the same shape and variance.\\
    $38.5>26\implies\text{ do not reject }H_0$
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $\text{H}_0:\eta_\text{ohm}=\eta_\text{watts}$ and $\text{H}_1:\eta_\text{ohm}\ne\eta_\text{watts}$
        \item $W=70$
        \item Since $70>71$ reject $H_0$ at the 5\% level of significance. There is evidence to suggest that electric cars from Ohm-G Motors and Watts-Up Motors have a different mean driving range.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex172a}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex172q]{Exercise 17.2}}{Exercise 17.2}}\label{exercise-17.2-1}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 17.2}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $-3.45<-1.64\implies\text{ reject }H_0$
        \item Assume that the population distributions of number of keepers for each weather condition have the same shape and variance.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item $-2.08>-2.58\implies\text{ do not reject }H_0$
    \item $-2.78<-1.64\implies\text{ reject }H_0$ at the 5\% level
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item The distributions of number of minor faults for each school appear to have the same shape and variance, so a Mann-Whitney test is appropriate. The distributions do not appear to be normal, so a $t$-test would not be appropriate.
        \item $-2.39>-3.09\implies\text{ do not reject }H_0$
        \item This data only considers the number of minor faults for students who passed, and does not show the proportions of students who passed or failed from each school, which students are likely to want to know before choosing a driving school.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex173a}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex173q]{Exercise 17.3}}{Exercise 17.3}}\label{exercise-17.3-1}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 17.3}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item 56
        \item $\{1,2,3\},\{1,2,4\},\{1,2,5\},\{1,3,4\}$
        \item $\frac{4}{56}=\frac{1}{14}\text{ or }0.0714$
    \end{enumerate}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item 126
        \item $\{1,2,3,4\},\{1,2,3,5\},\{1,2,3,6\},\{1,2,4,5\}$
        \item $\frac{4}{126}=\frac{2}{63}\text{ or }0.0317$
    \end{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $1+2+4+8=15$
        \item $\{1,2,3,4\},\{1,2,3,5\},\{1,2,3,6\},\{1,2,3,7\},\{1,2,3,8\}$\\
        $\{1,2,3,9\},\{1,2,4,5\},\{1,2,4,6\},\{1,2,4,7\},\{1,2,4,8\}$\\
        $\{1,2,5,6\},\{1,2,5,7\},\{1,3,4,5\},\{1,3,4,6\},\{1,3,4,7\}$\\
        $\{1,3,5,6\},\{2,3,4,5\},\{2,3,4,6\}$
        \item Assume that the population distributions of reactions times for each group have the same shape and variance.
        \item $\frac{18}{330}=\frac{3}{55}\text{ or }0.0545$
        \item $0.0545>0.05\implies\text{ do not reject }H_0$
    \end{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $\frac{7}{495}=0.0141$
        \item $0.0141<0.1\implies\text{ reject }H_0$
        \item Assume that the samples of hockey and rugby players are random.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}

\protect\phantomsection\label{re17a}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[re17q]{Chapter 17 Review Exercise}}{Chapter 17 Review Exercise}}\label{chapter-17-review-exercise}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Chapter 17 Review Exercise}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item $34>29\implies\text{ do not reject }H_0$
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item The stem-and-leaf diagram shows that red wines seem to contain more alcohol on average than white wines.
        \item $-3.04<-1.64\implies\text{ reject }H_0$
        \item Since the stem-and-leaf diagram seems to suggest that the distributions of alcohol content for the two groups have the same shape and variance, a Mann-Whitney test would be appropriate. Since the distributions both appear to be normal-like, a $t$-test would also be appropriate.
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $H_0:\;\eta_A = \eta_B$ and $H_0:\;\eta_A \ne \eta_B$
        \item CV$=13$. Since $12<13$ reject $H_0$ at the 2\% significance level, so there is evidence to suggest that the two populations do not have the same median.
        \item Assuming that the samples are random, and assume that the populations have the same shape and variance.
        \item The data for samples A and B will have been combined and ordered, with ranks assigned to each value. The rank sum for sample A will have been found, $W_A$, and then the value $4\times (4+9+1)-W_A$ will have also been calculated. \texttt{W=12} will have been the smaller of those two values.
    \end{enumerate}
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}

\endgroup

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex183a}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex183q]{Exercise 18.3}}{Exercise 18.3}}\label{exercise-18.3-1}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 18.3}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item $R^2=0.775$. This suggests that 77.5\% of the variation in $Y$ is explained by the linear model.
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $R^2=0.734$. This suggests that 73.4\% of the variation in the exmployees' salaries is explained by the linear model.\newline (Or: \textit{...by the number of years they have been employed for}).
        \item $R^2=0.573$. This suggests that 57.3\% of the variation in red squirrel mass is explained by the linear model.\newline (Or: \textit{...by the height of the red squirrels}).
        \item $R^2=0.449$. This suggests that 44.9\% of the variation in the top speed reached is explained by the linear model.\newline (Or: \textit{...by their lap time}).
    \end{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item The scatterplot appears to show a moderate/strong, positive linear association between a camera's shutter count and age.
        \item The correlation coefficient also suggests there is a moderate/strong, positive linear association between a camera's shutter count and its age.
        \item $R^2=0.516$. This suggests that 51,6\% of the variation in a camera's shutter count is explained by the linear model.\newline (Or: \textit{...by its age}).
        \item Since the data collected only includes digital cameras from within the last 12 years, any results from it may not apply to much older film cameras.\newline Also, the regression line is $y$ on $x$, suitable only for predicting shutter count based on a camera's age.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}

\pagebreak

\begingroup
\footnotesize

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex184a}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex184q]{Exercise 18.4}}{Exercise 18.4}}\label{exercise-18.4-1}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 18.4}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $\text{H}_0$: $\beta=0$ \newline $\text{H}_1$: $\beta\ne 0$
        \item $s^2 = \dfrac{224.2}{27-2}=8.968,\:s = \sqrt{8.987}=2.995,\:b = \dfrac{-129.6}{87.8}=-1.476,\:t = \dfrac{-1.476\times \sqrt{87.8}}{2.995}=-4.618$
        \item $\nu=27-2=25$
        \item Critical value for $\nu=25$ and a 5\% two tailed test (and a negative test statistic) is $t_{25,0.975}=-2.060$.\newline Since $-4.618<-2.060$ reject $\text{H}_0$ at the 5\% level of significance.\newline There is evidence to suggest that the slope parameter $\beta\ne 0$, and so the linear model is useful for prediction.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item Test statistic $t=-5.120$, critical value $t_{32,0.95}=-1.694$, reject $H_0$ since $-5.120<-1.694$.
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $\text{H}_0$: $\beta=0$ \newline $\text{H}_1$: $\beta\ne 0$
        \item $n=40$
        \item Critical value $t_{0.975,38}=-2.024$. Since $-0.42295>-2.024$, do not rejecrt $H_0$ at the 5\% significance level. There is insufficient evidence to suggest that the slope parameter $\beta$ is not equal to zero, and so no evidence that the linear model is useful for prediction.
        \item Assume that the population of errors are independent and identically distributed as $\text{N}(0,\sigma^2)$.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item Test statistic $t=-13.458$, critical value $t_{68,0.995}=-2.576$, reject $H_0$ since $-13.458<-2.576$.
    \item Test statistic $t=0.013$, critical value $t_{6,0.975}=2.447$, do not reject $H_0$ at the 5\% level since $0.013<2.447$.
\end{enumerate}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex185a}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex185q]{Exercise 18.5}}{Exercise 18.5}}\label{exercise-18.5-1}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 18.5}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $\text{H}_0$: $\rho=0$ \newline $\text{H}_1$: $\rho\ne 0$
        \item 
        \begin{flalign*}
            t &= \dfrac{-0.678\sqrt{27-2}}{\sqrt{1-(-0.678)^2}}=-4.612&&
        \end{flalign*}
        \item $\nu=27-2=25$
        \item Critical value for $\nu=25$ and a 5\% two tailed test (and a negative test statistic) is $t_{25,0.975}=-2.060$.\newline Since $-4.612<-2.060$ reject $\text{H}_0$ at the 5\% level of significance.\newline There is evidence to suggest that there is a linear association between the variables.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item Test statistic $t=6.418$, critical value $t_{12,0.95}=1.782$, reject $H_0$ at the 10\% level since $6.418>1.782$.
    \item Test statistic $t=1.683$, critical value $t_{12,0.995}=3.055$, do not reject $H_0$ at the 1\% level since $1.683<3.055$.
    \item Test statistic $t=-2.898$, critical value $t_{18,0.975}=-2.101$, reject $H_0$ at the 5\% level since $-2.898<-2.101$.\newline Assume that electrolytes amount and time are independent and follow approximately bivariate normal distributions.
    \item Test statistic $t=0.013$, critical value $t_{6,0.975}=2.447$, do not reject $H_0$ at the 5\% level since $0.013<2.447$.
\end{enumerate}

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex186a}{}

        \item $\hat{X}=7.709-0.0367y$
        \item 5.14 hours

\end{enumerate}

    \item The residual plot appears to show the points in the pattern of a curve ($\text{E}(\epsilon)\ne0$) with constant variance ($\text{V}(\epsilon)=\sigma^2$), which suggests that the linear regression model is not suitable.

    \item The residual plot appears to show the points centred on zero ($\text{E}(\epsilon)=0$) in a funnel pattern with increasing variance as fitted values increase ($\text{V}(\epsilon)\ne\sigma^2$), which suggests that the linear regression model is not suitable.
    \item The residual plot shows a random scatter of points not centred on zero ($\text{E}(\epsilon)\ne0$) with constant variance ($\text{V}(\epsilon)=\sigma^2$), which suggests the linear regression model is not suitable.
    \item The residual plot shows a random scatter of points centred on zero ($\text{E}(\epsilon)=0$) with constant variance ($\text{V}(\epsilon)=\sigma^2$), which suggests the linear regression model is suitable.
    \item The residual plot shows a random scatter of points centred on zero ($\text{E}(\epsilon)=0$) with constant variance ($\text{V}(\epsilon)=\sigma^2$), which suggests the linear regression model is suitable.
    \item The residual plot appears to show the points in the pattern of a curve ($\text{E}(\epsilon)\ne0$), which suggests that the linear regression model is not suitable.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item The residual plot appears to show the points form a curved pattern ($\text{E}(\epsilon)=0$) with increasing variance as fitted values increase ($\text{V}(\epsilon)\ne\sigma^2$), which suggests that the linear regression model is not suitable.
    \item The residual plot shows a random scatter of points centred on zero ($\text{E}(\epsilon)=0$) with constant variance ($\text{V}(\epsilon)=\sigma^2$), which suggests the linear regression model is suitable.
    \item The residual plot appears to show the points in the pattern of a curve ($\text{E}(\epsilon)\ne0$), which suggests that the linear regression model is not suitable.

\protect\phantomsection\label{ex188a}{}

\subsubsection*{\texorpdfstring{\hyperref[ex188q]{Exercise 18.8}}{Exercise 18.8}}\label{exercise-18.8-1}
\addcontentsline{toc}{subsubsection}{{Exercise 18.8}}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item There appears to be a strong negative linear association between `distance' and `$\sqrt{\text{power}}$'.\newline The coefficient of determination suggests 80.5\% of the variation in `$\sqrt{\text{power}}$' is explained by the model.
        \item There appears to be a weak positive linear association between `rating' and `$\log_{10}\text{energy}$'.\newline The coefficient of determination suggests 38.2\% of the variation in `$\log_2{\text{energy}}$' is explained by the model.
        \item There appears to be a very strong positive linear association between `$\log_2\text{time}$' and `$\log_2\text{population}$'.\newline The coefficient of determination suggests 97.4\% of the variation in `$\log_2{\text{population}}$' is explained by the model.
        \item There appears to be no linear association between `quality' and `$\dfrac{1}{\text{cost}}$'.\newline The coefficient of determination suggests 0.1\% of the variation in `$\dfrac{1}{\text{cost}}$' is explained by the model.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $\widehat{\dfrac{1}{Y}}=2.936+2.898x$
        \item 0.0574
    \end{enumerate}
    \item $r=-0.984$. This suggests that there is a very strong negative linear correlation between $\log x$ and $\log y$.
    \item 
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item They would have observed the points not showing an equal variance as fitted values increased, such as a funnel shape.
        \item They should use model C, as it is the only one whose residual plot appears to show a random scatter of points centred on zero, with a constant variance as fitted values increase. This suggests it is the only model of the three which is appropriate.
    \end{enumerate}
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item The residual plot appears to show a curved pattern, suggesting a linear model between $p$ and $x$ is not appropriate.
        \item The residual plot for this new model appears to show a random scatter of points centred around zero, with a constant variance, so this model appears to be appropriate.
        \item $\hat{Y}=0.0598+8.85x$
        \item 10.3
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}

\begin{enumerate}
    \item
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item 51.46
        \item Since $4.380>1.812$ reject $H_0$.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $R^2=0.300$. This suggests that 30.0\% of the variation in the tempo of a pop-punk song is explained by the linear model.
        \item Since $-2.620<-2.921$ do not reject $H_0$.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item 598.9
        \item (57.58,169.46)
        \item They are interested in a range of values for the number of daily visitors they might receive, rather than the mean number of daily visitors received by websites spending the same amount.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item The residual plot shows a curved pattern of points, suggesting that the linear model of boiling point on barometric pressure is not appropriate.
        \item A transformation could be taken on one or both of the variables and a new linear model created between those transformed variables.
    \end{enumerate}
    \item
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item $r=0.436$, suggesting that there is a moderate positive linear association between the distance a route covers and the ascent.
        \item The regression line given is a $y$ on $x$ regression line, suitable for predicting the ascent of a route given its distance. To predict the distance given the ascent, an $x$ on $y$ regression line would be needed.
        \item $\hat{X}=2.73+0.00539y$\newline
        predicted distance $=5.05$ miles
        \item This data has only been taken from the Ochil Hills, and any results from this data may not be relevant for routes in the Lomond Hills.
    \end{enumerate}
\end{enumerate}
